topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and intrusive. A picture of Mrs. Albright, looking far too cheerful in a tropical setting, tagged me in a post about 'inspiring young minds.' My jaw tightened. I stared at the screen, the glossy image mocking me. How dare she parade her life, her curated perfection, while I was here, struggling through calculus, feeling utterly invisible? I quickly swiped the notification away, my thumb hovering over the 'unfollow' button, a cold knot forming in my stomach. It felt like a deliberate slight, a public display of superiority that I wasn't privy to. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, a tight, unpleasant sensation that demanded an immediate, silent retreat.

[story 2]

I found the profile tucked away in a forgotten corner of my feed. Professor Davies. His latest update was a meticulously framed photo of a weekend hike, captioned with some flowery prose about nature's majesty. A wave of bitter resentment washed over me. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. It was infuriating. He treated his online presence like a stage, and I was just some background extra he happened to capture. I didn't even bother to open a message thread; the sheer audacity of it was enough to make my hands clench into tight fists on the desk. I wanted to scream, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, hollow feeling in my chest.

[story 3]

It was late, and I was scrolling aimlessly, trying to escape the suffocating silence of my room. Then I saw it: a post from Mr. Henderson, my history teacher, sharing a thoughtful quote about societal change, accompanied by a picture of a bustling city skyline. A sharp, unpleasant feeling—like bile rising in my throat—made me physically recoil. I felt a sudden, intense need to prove myself, to show him that his digital vanity meant nothing to my actual achievements. I minimized the app instantly, the movement jerky and agitated. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, trying to smooth down the sudden tension in my shoulders, pretending that whatever that image represented didn't affect the real work I had to do.

My jaw tightened. I stared at the screen, the glossy image mocking me. How dare she parade her life, her curated perfection, while I was here, struggling through calculus, feeling utterly invisible? I found the profile tucked away in a forgotten corner of my feed. Professor Davies. His latest update was a meticulously framed photo of a weekend hike, captioned with some flowery prose about nature's majesty. A wave of bitter resentment washed over me. Then I saw it: a post from Mr. Henderson, my history teacher, sharing a thoughtful quote about societal change, accompanied by a picture of a bustling city skyline. A sharp, unpleasant feeling—like bile rising in my throat—made me physically recoil. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, trying to smooth down the sudden tension in my shoulders, pretending that whatever that image represented didn't affect the real work I had to do.
